result.markdown:
--- title: "Lab 3: ANCOVA and Statistical Control" --- ::: {.objectives} ## What You'll Learn in This Lab - Prepare a two-group analysis dataset from `gcse` - Compare raw group means with a simple regression model - Fit an ANCOVA model with one categorical predictor and one continuous covariate - Interpret "holding a covariate constant" in plain language - Compute adjusted means with `modelbased::estimate_means()` - Compare unadjusted and adjusted group differences - Check collinearity with `performance::check_collinearity()` - **Lecture Notes Reference:** Chapter 2: *ANCOVA* ::: {.meta} Dataset: `gcse`. Estimated time: 75-90 minutes. ::: ::: ## Part 1: Data Preparation In Chapter 2, the motivating question is: > Do two schools differ in GCSE scores after accounting for students' earlier LRT scores? We will focus on two schools from the `gcse` dataset: - **Battersea**: school code `15` - **Chelsea**: school code `6` ### Step 1: Load the Data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} data(gcse) gcse_raw <- gcse ``` ::: As in the earlier labs, we keep the original data in a raw object and create a clean analysis object separately. ### Step 2: Create the Two-School Dataset ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} two_schools <- gcse_raw |> filter(school %in% c(15, 6)) |> mutate( school_name = factor( if_else(school == 15, "Battersea", "Chelsea"), levels = c("Battersea", "Chelsea") ) ) ``` ::: The reference group is Battersea because it is the first factor level. ### Step 3: Preflight Check ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} two_schools |> summarise( students = n(), schools = n_distinct(school_name), missing_gcse = sum(is.na(gcse)), missing_lrt = sum(is.na(lrt)), min_lrt = round(min(lrt, na.rm = TRUE), 2), max_lrt = round(max(lrt, na.rm = TRUE), 2), mean_lrt = round(mean(lrt, na.rm = TRUE), 2) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| students| schools| missing_gcse| missing_lrt| min_lrt| max_lrt| mean_lrt| |--------:|-------:|------------:|-----------:|-------:|-------:|--------:| | 171| 2| 0| 0| -14.47| 23.55| 4.42|
::: ::: The outcome is `gcse`, the age-16 exam score. The covariate is `lrt`, an earlier reading test score measured at age 11. ### Step 4: Raw Means by School ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} two_schools |> group_by(school_name) |> summarise( n = n(), mean_gcse = round(mean(gcse, na.rm = TRUE), 2), mean_lrt = round(mean(lrt, na.rm = TRUE), 2), .groups = "drop" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|school_name | n| mean_gcse| mean_lrt| |:-----------|--:|---------:|--------:| |Battersea | 91| -0.41| 2.70| |Chelsea | 80| 9.45| 6.38|
::: ::: This table shows the core confounding pattern: Chelsea has a higher raw GCSE mean, but Chelsea also has a higher mean LRT score at intake. ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [R]{.badge-r} Use the raw summary table to write two short sentences: 1. Which school has the higher raw GCSE mean? 2. Which school has the higher mean LRT score?
Hint The second sentence is the clue that LRT may be a confounder.
::: ## Part 2: Visualize the Confounding Pattern ### Raw GCSE Scores ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(two_schools, aes(x = school_name, y = gcse, fill = school_name)) + geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.75, show.legend = FALSE) + ber640_fill + labs(x = "School", y = "GCSE Score") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Raw GCSE scores by school](lab03-anova-ancova_files/figure-html/lab03-boxplot-gcse-school-1.png){fig-alt='Boxplot comparing raw GCSE scores for Battersea and Chelsea, with Chelsea having a higher median GCSE score' width=2100} ::: ::: ### GCSE and LRT Together ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(two_schools, aes(x = lrt, y = gcse, color = school_name)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.55, size = 2) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) + ber640_palette + labs(x = "LRT Score at Age 11", y = "GCSE Score at Age 16", color = "School") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![GCSE vs. LRT by school](lab03-anova-ancova_files/figure-html/lab03-scatter-lrt-gcse-by-school-1.png){fig-alt='Scatterplot of GCSE versus LRT for Battersea and Chelsea, with separate upward-sloping regression lines; Chelsea students tend to have higher LRT scores and higher GCSE scores' width=2100} ::: ::: The two lines are roughly parallel. That makes ANCOVA a reasonable first model: one common slope for LRT, plus one adjusted school difference. ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [R]{.badge-r} Look at the scatterplot and decide whether the lines look roughly parallel. Write one sentence saying whether the ANCOVA equal-slopes assumption seems reasonable as a starting point. ::: ## Part 3: Fit the Models ANCOVA compares schools while holding LRT constant. To see what adjustment changes, we fit two models: 1. An **unadjusted** model with school only. 2. An **adjusted** ANCOVA model with school and LRT. ### Step 1: Unadjusted School Difference ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_unadjusted <- lm(gcse ~ school_name, data = two_schools) model_params(model_unadjusted) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| t| df_error| p| |:------------------|-----------:|---------:|----:|---------:|---------:|----------:|--------:|---------:| |(Intercept) | -0.4060706| 0.9478704| 0.95| -2.277262| 1.465121| -0.4284031| 169| 0.6689032| |school_nameChelsea | 9.8517663| 1.3858046| 0.95| 7.116049| 12.587484| 7.1090587| 169| 0.0000000|
::: ::: The Chelsea coefficient is the raw mean difference in GCSE scores between Chelsea and Battersea. ### Step 2: Adjusted School Difference ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_ancova <- lm(gcse ~ lrt + school_name, data = two_schools) model_params(model_ancova) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| t| df_error| p| |:------------------|-----------:|---------:|----:|----------:|----------:|---------:|--------:|---------:| |(Intercept) | -2.125934| 0.7946062| 0.95| -3.6946341| -0.5572345| -2.675457| 168| 0.0082012| |lrt | 0.636308| 0.0685007| 0.95| 0.5010749| 0.7715411| 9.289070| 168| 0.0000000| |school_nameChelsea | 7.514195| 1.1574402| 0.95| 5.2291937| 9.7991965| 6.492081| 168| 0.0000000|
::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} In the ANCOVA model, `lrt` is the covariate. The `school_nameChelsea` coefficient compares Chelsea and Battersea students with the **same LRT score**. That is what "holding LRT constant" means in this model. ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [Write]{.badge-r} Interpret the `school_nameChelsea` coefficient from `model_ancova` in one sentence. Your sentence should use the phrase "after accounting for LRT" or "holding LRT constant."
Hint Start with: "After accounting for LRT, Chelsea students are predicted to..."
::: ::: {.pitfall} Do not write that Chelsea **causes** higher GCSE scores. This is observational school data, so the adjusted difference is a model-adjusted comparison. It is not automatic proof of a causal school effect. ::: ### Step 3: Model Fit ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_fit(model_ancova) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| AIC| BIC| R2| R2_adjusted| RMSE| |--------:|----:|--------:|-----------:|--------:| | 1173.433| 1186| 0.491418| 0.4853634| 7.306471|
::: ```{.r .cell-code} performance::r2(model_ancova) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # R2 for Linear Regression R2: 0.491 adj. R2: 0.485 ``` ::: ::: The ANCOVA model should explain more variation in GCSE than the school-only model because LRT is strongly related to later GCSE performance. ### Step 4: Compare the School Coefficient ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gap_comparison <- bind_rows( tidy_model(model_unadjusted) |> filter(term == "school_nameChelsea") |> mutate(model = "Unadjusted"), tidy_model(model_ancova) |> filter(term == "school_nameChelsea") |> mutate(model = "Adjusted for LRT") ) |> select(model, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |> mutate( across(c(estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high), \(x) round(x, 3)), p.value = if_else(p.value < .001, "< .001", as.character(round(p.value, 3))) ) gap_comparison ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|model | estimate| std.error| conf.low| conf.high|p.value | |:----------------|--------:|---------:|--------:|---------:|:-------| |Unadjusted | 9.852| 1.386| 7.116| 12.588|< .001 | |Adjusted for LRT | 7.514| 1.157| 5.229| 9.799|< .001 |
::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} The adjusted estimate is smaller than the unadjusted estimate. This tells us that part of the raw Chelsea-Battersea GCSE gap reflects differences in LRT scores at intake. This is a model-adjusted comparison, not automatic proof that school attendance caused the remaining gap. ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [R]{.badge-r} Use `gap_comparison` to compute how much the Chelsea-Battersea estimate changed after adding LRT. Then write one sentence explaining what the change suggests.
Hint Subtract the adjusted estimate from the unadjusted estimate. A positive difference means the raw gap was larger than the adjusted gap.
::: ## Part 4: Adjusted Means Adjusted means answer this question: > What GCSE score does the model predict for each school if both schools are compared at the same representative LRT value? ### Step 1: Estimate Adjusted Means ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} adjusted_means <- modelbased::estimate_means(model_ancova, by = "school_name") adjusted_means ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|school_name | Mean| SE| CI_low| CI_high| t| df| |:-----------|--------:|---------:|----------:|--------:|---------:|---:| |Battersea | 0.687530| 0.7816515| -0.8555949| 2.230655| 0.8795863| 168| |Chelsea | 8.201725| 0.8349590| 6.5533614| 9.850089| 9.8229073| 168|
::: ::: The adjusted means are model-predicted means for each school after accounting for LRT. Here, both schools are evaluated at the same representative LRT value: the sample mean, about 4.42. ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [R]{.badge-r} Compare the adjusted means with the raw means. Which school moved upward or downward after adjustment?
Hint Use the raw school summary from Part 1 and the `adjusted_means` output above; compare `mean_gcse` with the adjusted mean within each school.
::: ### Step 2: Estimate the Adjusted Contrast ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} modelbased::estimate_contrasts(model_ancova, contrast = "school_name") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Level1 |Level2 | Difference| SE| CI_low| CI_high| t| df| p| |:-------|:---------|----------:|-------:|--------:|--------:|-------:|---:|--:| |Chelsea |Battersea | 7.514195| 1.15744| 5.229194| 9.799196| 6.49208| 168| 0|
::: ::: The adjusted contrast equals the `school_nameChelsea` coefficient from `model_ancova`. Because `Level1` is Chelsea and `Level2` is Battersea, the contrast is Chelsea minus Battersea. ### Step 3: Raw vs. Adjusted Means ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} raw_means <- two_schools |> group_by(school_name) |> summarise(raw_mean = mean(gcse, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") adjusted_means_tbl <- adjusted_means |> as_tibble() |> select(school_name, adjusted_mean = Mean) left_join(raw_means, adjusted_means_tbl, by = "school_name") |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|school_name | raw_mean| adjusted_mean| |:-----------|--------:|-------------:| |Battersea | -0.41| 0.69| |Chelsea | 9.45| 8.20|
::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} The raw means are observed school averages. The adjusted means are model predictions at the same representative LRT value. They answer a comparison question, not a causal question. ::: ### Step 4: Plot Raw and Adjusted Means ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} means_plot <- bind_rows( raw_means |> rename(mean_score = raw_mean) |> mutate(type = "Raw mean"), adjusted_means_tbl |> rename(mean_score = adjusted_mean) |> mutate(type = "Adjusted mean") ) |> mutate(type = factor(type, levels = c("Raw mean", "Adjusted mean"))) ggplot(means_plot, aes(x = type, y = mean_score, color = school_name, group = school_name)) + geom_line(linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) + geom_point(size = 4) + ber640_palette + labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean GCSE Score", color = "School") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Raw and LRT-adjusted mean GCSE scores](lab03-anova-ancova_files/figure-html/lab03-raw-adjusted-plot-1.png){fig-alt='Dot and line plot comparing raw and LRT-adjusted mean GCSE scores for Battersea and Chelsea, showing that the school gap becomes smaller after adjustment' width=2100} ::: ::: ## Part 5: Model Comparison and Collinearity ### Step 1: Compare Model Performance ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} compare_models(model_unadjusted, model_ancova) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Name |Model | R2| R2_adjusted| RMSE| AIC_wt| BIC_wt| Performance_Score| |:----|:-----|---------:|-----------:|--------:|------:|------:|-----------------:| |..2 |lm | 0.4914180| 0.4853634| 7.306471| 1| 1| 1| |..1 |lm | 0.2302041| 0.2256491| 8.989073| 0| 0| 0|
::: ::: The ANCOVA model should have higher $R^2$ and lower AIC/BIC than the school-only model. ### Step 2: Nested Model Test ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} anova(model_unadjusted, model_ancova) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Res.Df| RSS| Df| Sum of Sq| F| Pr(>F)| |------:|---------:|--:|---------:|--------:|------:| | 169| 13817.389| NA| NA| NA| NA| | 168| 9128.752| 1| 4688.637| 86.28682| 0|
::: ::: This test asks whether adding LRT improves the model beyond school alone. ### Step 3: Check the Parallel-Slopes Assumption The ANCOVA model assumes that the LRT slope is the same in both schools. We can check that assumption by comparing the ANCOVA model with an interaction model: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_interaction <- lm(gcse ~ lrt * school_name, data = two_schools) anova(model_ancova, model_interaction) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Res.Df| RSS| Df| Sum of Sq| F| Pr(>F)| |------:|--------:|--:|---------:|--------:|--------:| | 168| 9128.752| NA| NA| NA| NA| | 167| 9013.457| 1| 115.2955| 2.136178| 0.145739|
::: ::: This test asks whether allowing the LRT slope to differ by school improves the model. A non-significant result does not prove the slopes are exactly equal, but it supports using the simpler parallel-lines ANCOVA model for this lab. ### Step 4: Check Collinearity ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} performance::check_collinearity(model_ancova) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Term | VIF| VIF_CI_low| VIF_CI_high| SE_factor| Tolerance| Tolerance_CI_low| Tolerance_CI_high| |:-----------|--------:|----------:|-----------:|---------:|---------:|----------------:|-----------------:| |lrt | 1.049616| 1.001993| 2.235509| 1.024507| 0.9527297| 0.4473253| 0.9980115| |school_name | 1.049616| 1.001993| 2.235509| 1.024507| 0.9527297| 0.4473253| 0.9980115|
::: ::: ::: {.assumption-check} Low VIF values mean that `lrt` and `school_name` are not so redundant that they make the coefficients unstable. In later labs with more predictors, collinearity checks will become more important. ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [R]{.badge-r} Use the parallel-slopes test and the collinearity table to write one diagnostic note. Your note should mention whether the interaction test and VIF table support the simple ANCOVA model as a reasonable working model. ::: ::: {.exercises} ## What to Submit 1. The raw school summary table with `mean_gcse` and `mean_lrt`. [core]{.chip .easy} 2. The coefficient table for `model_ancova`. [core]{.chip .easy} 3. The `gap_comparison` table comparing the unadjusted and LRT-adjusted school coefficient. [core]{.chip .med} 4. The raw vs. adjusted means table or plot. [core]{.chip .med} 5. One diagnostic note using the parallel-slopes test and `performance::check_collinearity()` output. [core]{.chip .med} 6. A 4-sentence interpretation answering the research question. Use model-adjusted language and avoid causal claims. [core]{.chip .hard} ::: ::: {.summary} ## Lab Summary 1. ANCOVA is a regression model with both a categorical predictor and a continuous covariate. 2. The unadjusted school coefficient is the raw group mean difference. 3. The adjusted school coefficient compares schools at the same LRT level. 4. `modelbased::estimate_means()` gives adjusted means, and `modelbased::estimate_contrasts()` gives adjusted group differences. 5. `compare_models()` and `anova()` show whether the covariate improves model fit. 6. A quick interaction test checks whether the parallel-slopes ANCOVA is a reasonable working model. 7. `performance::check_collinearity()` checks whether predictors are too redundant for stable coefficient interpretation. **Next:** Lab 4 moves the one-way ANOVA and categorical-predictor material into its own focused workflow. :::
